```{=latex}
\ifdefined\ArchTwoReviewBuild
\mainmatter
\fi
```

# The Architecture 2.0 Moonshot {#sec-moonshot}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"The purpose of computing is insight, not numbers."*
>
> — Richard Hamming, *Numerical Methods for Scientists and Engineers* (1962)
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** Richard Hamming, a pioneer in computer science, argued that
computing should yield insight rather than raw data. The distinction matters
here. AI-assisted architecture should help architects ask better questions,
test alternatives, explain mechanisms, and defend decisions. Producing more
candidates is useful only when it improves that work.
:::

```{=latex}
\abstract*{This chapter introduces the Architecture 2.0 moonshot and the lighthouse design request used throughout the book. It follows the historical progression from device scaling to parallelism and specialization, then asks how AI can assist the architecture work itself. The aim is not push-button chip design, but AI participation that helps an architect formulate, explore, implement, evaluate, explain, and defend a system decision with evidence.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
What would it take for AI to help an architect formulate, explore, implement,
evaluate, explain, and defend a system design with evidence, rather than merely
generate a plausible candidate?
:::

This opening chapter introduces the book's central proposition. AI should help
architects carry out the work that connects a design goal to a defensible
decision, not merely produce more artifacts. The lighthouse request provides a
running target for that ambition. This chapter explains why the target matters,
what it includes, and where the argument stops.
@sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales examines where current architecture work is
under strain, and @sec-architecture-20-ontology defines the structure an
AI-assisted study needs.

For roughly fifty years, processor generations improved through a familiar
progression. Device scaling regularly delivered faster, cheaper, and more
energy-efficient transistors. Quantitative analysis gave architects a way to
compare the resulting design choices. When scaling slowed, microarchitecture,
parallelism, and eventually domain specialization provided more of the gains
[@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach; @HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge].
The change is visible in fifty years of
frontier microprocessor data. Clock frequency and typical power flatten in the
mid-2000s, while transistor counts continue to rise and core counts turn upward
(@fig-microprocessor-trends).

```{python}
#| label: fig-microprocessor-trends
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Frontier microprocessor trends from 1971 to 2021.** Data from Karl Rupp's public microprocessor dataset are summarized as the highest value in each four-year interval [@Rupp2022MicroprocessorTrendData]. Clock frequency and typical power flatten in the mid-2000s as Dennard scaling ends, while transistor counts continue to rise and core counts turn upward [@DennardEtAl1974Scaling; @EsmaeilzadehEtAl2011DarkSilicon]. The figure shows frontier values rather than every processor; the era boundaries are approximate [@HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge].
#| out-width: "100%"
#| fig-alt: "Log-scale line chart of frontier microprocessor trends from 1971 to 2021 with five series. Transistors per chip rise from a few thousand to tens of billions by 2021. Single-thread performance rises steeply until the mid-2000s and then slows. Clock frequency plateaus near 3 to 5 GHz and typical power near 100 to 500 W after about 2005, while logical core counts climb from one to the hundreds. Dashed vertical lines mark the approximate end of Dennard scaling around 2005 and the specialization turn around 2016. A dotted boundary past 2025 marks AI-assisted compositional design as a prospective era rather than a measured trend."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, add_note_box, apply_style

apply_style()

# Fifty-year microprocessor trend data; per-series frontier points
# (per-4-year-bucket maxima) transcribed from the public dataset
# github.com/karlrupp/microprocessor-trend-data (50yrs/*.dat, CC-BY 4.0),
# which compiles the Horowitz/Labonte/Shacham/Olukotun/Hammond/Batten
# collection through 2010 plus Rupp's additions through 2021. Values are
# dataset-transcribed, not recomputed; fractional years as in the dataset;
# transistors in thousands, single-thread performance in SpecINT x 1000.
# Era-boundary annotations (~2005 Dennard end, ~2016 specialization turn)
# are approximate: [@DennardEtAl1974Scaling; @EsmaeilzadehEtAl2011DarkSilicon;
# @HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge]. Accessed 2026-07-14.
# Receipt: data/source-receipts/chapter1-micro-trend.csv
series = [
    {
        "label": "Transistors\n(thousands)",
        "color": COLORS["purple"],
        "points": [(1974.3, 6.1), (1982.3, 136), (1985.9, 274), (1989.5, 1210),
                   (1992.7, 3110), (1998.9, 15300), (2002.9, 221000),
                   (2007.0, 583000), (2009.7, 2.31e6), (2014.8, 5.7e6),
                   (2018.5, 2.11e7), (2021.9, 5.82e7)],
        "label_y": 5.82e7,
    },
    {
        "label": "Single-thread perf\n(SpecINT x 1000)",
        "color": COLORS["blue"],
        "points": [(1988.7, 83.5), (1994.8, 599), (1998.9, 3550),
                   (2002.4, 9390), (2007.0, 35200), (2010.3, 42000),
                   (2014.8, 66000), (2017.6, 83600), (2021.3, 105000)],
        "label_y": 1.05e5,
    },
    {
        "label": "Frequency (MHz)",
        "color": COLORS["orange"],
        "points": [(1974.3, 2.02), (1982.3, 6.1), (1985.9, 16.1),
                   (1988.7, 40.3), (1994.7, 297), (1997.5, 538),
                   (2002.4, 2250), (2004.9, 3650), (2007.7, 4660),
                   (2012.7, 3700), (2016.7, 4000), (2020.9, 3200)],
        "label_y": 3.2e3,
    },
    {
        "label": "Typical power (W)",
        "color": COLORS["red"],
        "points": [(1974.3, 0.922), (1982.3, 3.02), (1986.2, 3.02),
                   (1988.7, 3.96), (1993.0, 35.2), (1998.9, 90.6),
                   (2001.7, 98.2), (2003.4, 155), (2010.4, 168),
                   (2013.4, 300), (2018.5, 250), (2021.9, 500)],
        "label_y": 7.5e2,
    },
    {
        "label": "Logical cores",
        "color": COLORS["green"],
        "points": [(1971.9, 1), (1979.6, 1), (1985.9, 1), (1988.7, 1),
                   (1992.4, 1), (1995.4, 1), (1999.2, 1), (2006.0, 8),
                   (2010.2, 16), (2013.4, 240), (2016.5, 272), (2021.9, 208)],
        "label_y": 1.1e2,
    },
]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.4, 3.6))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.075, right=0.775, top=0.90, bottom=0.225)

ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlim(1970, 2032.5)
ax.set_ylim(0.4, 3e9)

for x in (2005, 2016):
    ax.axvline(x, color=COLORS["muted"], linewidth=0.8,
               linestyle=(0, (4, 3)), zorder=1)

era_labels = [
    (1987.5, "Device-scaling era"),
    (2010.5, "Parallelism era"),
    (2019.6, "Specialization\nera"),
]
for x, text in era_labels:
    ax.text(x, 6.5e8, text, ha="center", va="center", fontsize=6.2,
            fontweight="bold", color=COLORS["ink"])

# Prospective era: annotation only, no data points and no band fill, styled
# like the other era boundaries but dotted to mark a prospective argument.
ax.axvline(2025, color=COLORS["muted"], linewidth=0.8,
           linestyle=(0, (1.5, 2.5)), zorder=1)
ax.text(2028.9, 6.5e8, "AI-assisted\ncompositional\ndesign?", ha="center",
        va="center", fontsize=5.4, fontweight="bold",
        color=COLORS["workload_ink"], linespacing=1.2)
ax.text(2028.9, 2.5e7, "(prospective,\nnot measured\ndata)", ha="center",
        va="top", fontsize=5.4, color=COLORS["muted"], linespacing=1.25)

ax.text(2004.3, 4e7, "Dennard scaling\nends", ha="right", va="center",
        fontsize=5.6, color=COLORS["muted"], linespacing=1.15)
ax.text(2016.4, 1.6e6, "specialization\nturn", ha="left", va="center",
        fontsize=5.6, color=COLORS["muted"], linespacing=1.15)

for s in series:
    xs = [p[0] for p in s["points"]]
    ys = [p[1] for p in s["points"]]
    ax.plot(xs, ys, color=s["color"], linewidth=1.6, zorder=2)
    ax.scatter(xs, ys, s=13, color=s["color"], zorder=3)
    ax.text(1.015, s["label_y"], s["label"], ha="left", va="center",
            fontsize=6.0, fontweight="bold", color=s["color"],
            clip_on=False, linespacing=1.15,
            transform=ax.get_yaxis_transform())

ax.set_xticks([1970, 1980, 1990, 2000, 2010, 2020])
ax.set_yticks([1, 1e2, 1e4, 1e6, 1e8])
ax.set_yticklabels([r"$10^0$", r"$10^2$", r"$10^4$", r"$10^6$", r"$10^8$"])
ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=6.5, length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
ax.grid(axis="y", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.6)

for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])

add_note_box(
    fig,
    "Four-year frontier maxima from Rupp's 50-year dataset; "
    "era boundaries are approximate.",
    xywh=(0.075, 0.03, 0.70, 0.075),
    fontsize=5.6,
)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

Specialization brought major advances, but it also multiplied the number of
architectures, software interfaces, and physical constraints that designers
must consider. The rise of machine learning accelerated this change. The
authors of Project Brainwave described the resulting spread of GPUs, FPGAs,
neural processors, and other specialized hardware as a "Cambrian explosion"
[@ChungEtAl2018Brainwave]. New dataflows, memory systems, and programming
interfaces appeared in quick succession [@HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge]. Much
of the coordination among workloads, compilers, simulators, RTL, and physical
design still falls to architects.

AI is now being applied to the design process that produced those accelerators,
including architectural design-space exploration and physical placement
[@WangEtAl2026AgentDSE; @MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement].
In this material, *AI* is an umbrella term rather than the name of one model
family. A study may combine several AI methods with simulators, compilers, EDA
tools, and checking tools. Those methods may generate mechanisms or edit
artifacts, predict behavior and risk, search design spaces, or choose
experiments. They may also critique assumptions, repair invalid artifacts, or
check results. Not every study needs all of these roles. One study may need more
promising alternatives, while another may be limited by simulator time,
physical-design runs, or the effort required to understand conflicting results.
@sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales examines those bottlenecks in detail. The
purpose here is broader. AI should improve the work needed to move from a design
request to a defensible decision rather than merely increase the amount of
output.

One design request makes that question concrete.

> **Lighthouse prompt.** Design a low-power, 64-bit RISC-V-based compute subsystem for an
> XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload. Realize it as a vector-capable CPU, accelerator, or SoC block under a 3 W TDP target in a 3 nm-class LP mobile process, and return a design-space report with evidence and rejected alternatives.

The request is deliberately audacious. No current system can answer it end to
end. Its scale exposes the architecture work that a smaller generation task
would hide and gives the material a long-term target for judging partial
progress.

This compact request is not yet an architecture study. An architect must still
define the workload and baseline, choose meaningful alternatives, connect them
to executable tools, and decide what evidence could support or overturn a
recommendation. AI may assist with each part. It may also produce candidate
designs, scripts, and summaries faster than architects and reviewers can
evaluate them or connect them to a decision.

Suppose a method proposes a thousand accelerators. If architects cannot test why
they differ, explain why most should be rejected, or state what would overturn
the apparent winner, the extra proposals have not advanced the architecture
study. A fast analytical model could still help by screening out designs that
exceed the thermal budget before they consume scarce simulator or
physical-design time. The value comes from reducing the expensive engineering
work, not from the proposal count itself.

We use the same request throughout this material. That lets later chapters
change the method or the part of the stack under study without quietly changing
the workload, constraints, or standard of evidence.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter, you can expand a compact architecture request into a
well-scoped
AI-assisted study. That means you can:

- **Context:** Distinguish established uses of machine learning in architecture from broader AI participation across an architecture study.
- **Formulate:** Define the lighthouse request in terms of workload, baseline, alternatives, constraints, and non-goals.
- **Explore and implement:** Identify meaningful alternatives and the executable artifacts and tool paths needed to compare them.
- **Evaluate and explain:** Specify experiments and observations that could support or overturn the result, then explain the architectural mechanisms involved.
- **Defend:** State a recommendation, its evidence limits, and the conditions that would reverse it.
- **Compare efficiency:** Treat efficiency as a multidimensional architecture claim rather than a single score.
:::

## Ask What AI Can Do for Architecture

AI in computer architecture is not new.
Researchers have used it for branch predictors, prefetchers, surrogate models,
reinforcement-learning controllers, design-space exploration, and autotuning.
Chen, Penney, and Jiménez survey this work component by component in *AI for
Computer Architecture: Principles, Practice, and Prospects*
[@ChenPenneyJimenez2020AIArchitecture]. Predictive design-space exploration and
tensor-program autotuning are two examples
[@IpekEtAl2006PredictiveDSE; @ChenEtAl2018AutoTVM]. In most of this work,
learning operates inside an artifact or performs a specific job within a study
defined by people.

Recent generative and tool-using AI systems can assist with a larger share of
the study. They may help
frame a problem, propose or edit mechanisms, call tools, choose experiments,
respond to simulator feedback, interpret results, and influence which
candidate reaches review. Generating text, writing scripts, summarizing papers,
and proposing configurations are visible examples, but they are only parts of
the work that connects an architecture question to a decision. Our scope
includes both learned components inside a design and AI systems used to conduct
the study. In either case, the important question is how the method changes the
evidence behind an architectural conclusion.

Architecture work has always moved through repeated analysis and revision.
Architects frame a problem, choose abstractions, construct models or
simulators, select workloads, explore alternatives, evaluate results, reject
weak candidates, and revise assumptions. Consider an architect weighing a
larger L2 cache for a stated workload mix. The architect might use gem5, a
cycle-level architecture simulator, or FireSim, an FPGA-accelerated full-system
RTL simulation platform, to sweep cache sizes and policies. They would then
compare miss rates, performance, and energy estimates. They would explain
whether locality, contention, or latency hiding caused the result and decide
whether the evidence supports a cache change. Simulators, compilers, profilers,
synthesis tools, spreadsheets, dashboards, and design reviews all mediate that
judgment.

It is clearer to describe the job an AI method performs than to group methods
only by model family. Generation proposes artifacts and candidates, prediction
estimates behavior and risk, and optimization searches tradeoffs under
constraints. Critique can expose a weak assumption, repair can correct an
invalid artifact, and verification can test whether a result passes the checks
defined for the study.
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization develops all six roles.

@fig-architecture-development-triad previews the three roles most often used to
produce and select candidates. The other three help challenge, correct, and
check that work.

![**Three method roles within one architecture study:** Generation proposes artifacts and candidates, prediction estimates behavior and risk, and optimization searches tradeoffs under constraints. The architect still needs evidence, a mechanism-level explanation, and judgment about the result.](images/F1b-architecture-development-triad){#fig-architecture-development-triad width="100%" fig-alt="Three-circle diagram showing generation, prediction, and optimization as interacting method jobs within a broader AI-assisted architecture study."}

These jobs need not occur in a fixed order. Generation can expose a missing
constraint. Prediction can show that the available evidence does not separate
two candidates. Optimization can allocate scarce simulator or EDA runs, while
critique and verification can disqualify the apparent winner. Repair changes
the artifact and may invalidate earlier measurements.

To evaluate any of these contributions, an architect must be able to see what
the system inspected and changed, how it obtained feedback, which observations
counted as evidence, and which checks could reject its result. The responsible
architect still sets the intent, chooses the abstraction and fidelity,
interprets the architectural mechanisms, decides what evidence is adequate,
and determines which decisions may be automated. The degree of
automation can vary, but the study should state which decisions AI may make and
which remain with people. Automating a step does not transfer responsibility
for the architectural conclusion.

AI-assisted architecture extends a long history of design automation, but it
begins from a broader statement of intent than earlier tools.

@fig-automation-ladder places this broader form of AI participation in the
history of design automation. The dates are approximate. Each stage accepts a
broader statement of intent and produces a larger artifact.

1. **1980s--1990s (Logic Synthesis):** Logic synthesis translated Boolean and RTL descriptions into gate-level structure under library, physical, and timing constraints [@DeMicheli1994SynthesisOptimization].
2. **1990s--2000s (High-Level Synthesis):** High-level synthesis generated RTL candidates from algorithmic descriptions and moved part of the tactical microarchitecture search into tools [@CoussyMorawiec2008HighLevelSynthesis].
3. **2000s--2010s (Domain-Specific Synthesis):** Building on deductive program synthesis from the 1970s, domain-specific synthesis generated executable code and specialized hardware/software artifacts from mathematical kernels and DSL descriptions [@GulwaniPolozovSingh2017ProgramSynthesis].
4. **2010s--2020s (Agile Hardware Generation):** Agile hardware generation used parameterized templates, such as Chisel and its FIRRTL intermediate representation, to create specialized SoC instances from reusable IP [@chipyard].
5. **2020s onward (AI-Assisted Architecture):** AI can help connect workload intent, software contracts, architectural alternatives, executable tools, experiments, and physical constraints inside a scoped study. The result should be a finding or decision supported by evidence, even when much of the study remains under human control.

![**Five stages of design automation.** Each stage accepts a broader statement of intent and produces a larger artifact, from Boolean and RTL descriptions that yield gate-level structure to a scoped architecture study that yields a finding or decision supported by evidence. Era spans are approximate and follow the literature cited above.](images/F2b-automation-ladder-timeline){#fig-automation-ladder width="100%" fig-alt="Staircase diagram of five design-automation stages rising from lower left to upper right, each box wider than the one below. From bottom to top: logic synthesis, high-level synthesis, domain-specific synthesis, agile hardware generation, and AI-assisted architecture. A horizontal arrow marks approximate time order and a vertical arrow marks the widening unit of intent and artifact."}

The earlier stages remain essential. Logic synthesis, for example, became
dependable because static timing analysis and equivalence checking could expose
bad netlists. AI-assisted architecture draws on these established tools while
connecting decisions made across stages. The study must identify which tool
produced each result and which assumptions limit how that result can be used.

The record for an iterative study should include the proposals considered, the
results obtained, the candidates rejected or revised, and the decisions that
followed. Different readers use this information for different reasons.

- **Researcher:** Describe an AI-for-architecture paper by naming the
  architecture question, the role AI played, the artifact or finding produced,
  and the evidence and limits behind it.
- **Tool builder:** Ask whether a harness keeps the inputs, settings,
  results, and failures that another method or engineer would need.
- **Author or reviewer:** Ask what would reject the claim, why the measured
  mechanism supports it, and which decision it can justify.

## From Architecture 1.0 to Architecture 2.0 {#sec-from-architecture-10-to-architecture-20}

These review questions apply whether people orchestrate every step or AI
participates in part of the work. Architecture 1.0 is shorthand for familiar,
human-orchestrated design. The
architect defines the problem, chooses models, uses tools, interprets feedback,
and decides what to build. Architecture 2.0 retains that practice while
allowing AI to take part in more of the study
[@ReddiYazdanbakhsh2025Architecture20].

As AI takes on more work, the study must identify the inputs and artifact
versions available to the system, the actions it may take, the feedback it
received, and the candidates it rejected. The architect remains responsible
for the intent, constraints, abstraction, evidence standard, interpretation,
and final decision. This information lets another architect determine what
happened and what the result can support.

Consider an architect deciding whether a larger L2 cache would help a stated
workload mix. In a human-orchestrated version, the architect scripts the sweep
and inspects the results. In an AI-assisted version, a method might propose
candidates, a surrogate[^fn-ml-surrogate-model] estimate outcomes, a simulator
evaluate selected points, and a critic flag invalid assumptions.
The architect still decides whether the evidence is strong enough. The
contribution might be a cache design, an architectural finding, a reusable
method or dataset, or a decision supported by the available evidence. The
proposals, tool results, and decisions show another architect how the
contribution was produced.

[^fn-ml-surrogate-model]: A surrogate model is a cheap, data-driven stand-in for an expensive simulator. In a study it lets a method screen many candidates quickly, but its score is only a proxy. A promising candidate still needs a higher-fidelity check before it can support a design decision.

@fig-design-loop-breaks shows the practical difference. In the left loop,
architectural judgment coordinates intent, models, candidates, tool runs, and
expert review. The right loop keeps that judgment but makes the inputs,
artifact versions, permitted actions, tool feedback, evidence, and rejected
candidates explicit when AI methods participate.

![**AI participation makes design state explicit.** Architects often carry design state and rationale informally. When AI participates, the study needs explicit inputs, artifact versions, permitted actions, tool feedback, evidence, and rejected candidates.](images/F2a-design-loop-breaks){#fig-design-loop-breaks width="100%" fig-alt="Two side-by-side loop diagrams compare a human-orchestrated architecture study with an AI-assisted study that makes inputs, artifact versions, permitted actions, tool feedback, evidence, and rejected candidates explicit."}

For each result, the study should identify the artifact version and tool
settings used, the evidence produced, and any decision made by an architect or
reviewer. A reviewer can then determine whether the work supports the proposed
claim.

::: {#pri-design-the-loop .callout-design-principle title="Evaluate the whole study"}
Do not judge a generated artifact in isolation. Review the question it
addressed, the alternatives considered, the actions the method could take, the
tool results, the rejected candidates, and the evidence behind the decision.
:::

An AI system that calls a simulator, writes RTL, or summarizes papers still
depends on sound systems engineering. Architects need to identify the likely
bottleneck, account for memory movement and physical limits, compare tradeoffs
numerically, and decide what evidence justifies the next step. They should also
keep source information and failed results so that reviewers can trace how the
study reached its conclusion.

Safety engineering offers a useful precedent. Reviewers retain system
assumptions, hazards, and supporting evidence so that later decisions can be
checked against the conditions on which they depended
[@Leveson2012EngineeringSaferWorld]. An architecture study needs the same care
with its inputs, tool settings, results, and failures.

These practices make an individual study easier to review. The larger question
is whether they can support AI across a much greater share of architecture
design.

## The Architecture Moonshot

Engineers use *moonshot* for an ambitious goal that requires more than
incremental improvement. X, the Moonshot Factory, describes it as the
intersection of a large problem, a radical solution, and a technological
breakthrough that makes progress plausible [@XCompanyMoonshotBlueprint]. The
full solution may remain distant, but the target should be specific enough to
organize experiments and show which advances matter.

Computer architecture has comparable precedents. The Mead and Conway VLSI
methodology[^fn-vlsi-complexity] joined abstractions and design rules to tools,
fabrication access, and pedagogy [@MeadConway1980VLSI]. The reduced-instruction-set program
reshaped the hardware/software contract around quantitative evidence
[@PattersonDitzel1980RISC]. Both efforts produced more than a single design.
They changed the methods architects used and the artifacts they could share.

[^fn-vlsi-complexity]: Early-1980s frontier microprocessors contained about $10^4$ to $10^5$ transistors. By 2021, frontier chips contained tens of billions [@Rupp2022MicroprocessorTrendData]. VLSI design was already outgrowing transistor-level practice. Mead and Conway made higher-level abstractions, design rules, and fabrication access part of a method that could be taught and shared.

> **Architecture moonshot.** An architecture moonshot is a difficult,
> long-term target that existing tools and design practice cannot yet
> address as a complete study. It seeks a major improvement in how architects
> use AI to formulate problems, explore and implement alternatives, evaluate
> and explain results, and defend decisions. Advances in AI, data, or tools
> must make at least part of the target practical to investigate without
> implying that the whole problem has been solved.

@fig-moonshot-venn separates the three conditions in the definition. The grand
challenge is architecture work that current, ad hoc coordination cannot handle
well. The radical target is a rigorous AI-assisted practice that changes how
architects formulate, explore, implement, evaluate, explain, and defend
designs. The enabling breakthrough is the combination of AI, data, and tools
that makes part of that target practical to investigate now. None of the three
is sufficient alone. Together, they identify an important architecture problem,
a different way to approach it, and a practical place to begin testing that
approach even though the complete goal remains out of reach.

![**Three conditions for an architecture moonshot:** The target combines a major architecture challenge, a substantial change in AI-assisted design work, and an advance in AI, data, or tools that makes part of the problem practical to study.](images/F1c-moonshot-venn){#fig-moonshot-venn width="100%" fig-alt="Three-circle Venn diagram showing an architecture moonshot at the intersection of a grand challenge, a radical practice target, and an enabling breakthrough."}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

The lighthouse request fits all three conditions. Today, architects coordinate
decisions across workloads, software, hardware, tools, and physical design. The
long-term aim is for AI to contribute across that work while the architect
remains responsible for the final claim. Advances in models, data, and tools
make parts of the approach practical now. The complete study remains beyond
present capability, which is why it is a moonshot rather than a near-term
product specification.

Complex systems often become simple at the point of use only after years of
engineering beneath the interface. A cloud console can launch a virtual server
in minutes. An online order can set off picking, packing, routing, and delivery
across a distributed fulfillment network. Neither action is intrinsically
simple. Each depends on layers of standardization, automation, monitoring, and
human operations. The lighthouse request asks what comparable representations,
interfaces, tools, evaluation methods, and oversight would be needed to make a
complete architecture study tractable.

To apply that ambition to a design request, we first need to say what
*architecture* and *architect* mean here. Architecture includes more than a
microarchitecture, block diagram, or chip artifact.

> **Architecture.** Architecture is the hardware-software contract and system
> organization that turn workload intent and technology constraints into a
> defensible system design. It includes instruction set architecture (ISA) and microarchitecture, memory and
> interconnect, accelerators and chiplets, compiler/runtime interfaces,
> physical-design constraints, verification, deployment, and the evidence used
> to justify choices.

> **Architect.** The architect is responsible for the decision. An architect
> frames the problem, chooses the abstractions, sets the evidence standard,
> defines what can reject a candidate, and remains answerable for the committed
> design. One person, an engineering team, or several authorized review roles
> may do this work. The study must still state who may make which decisions and
> who gives final approval. AI methods can contribute within the study, but the
> architect remains responsible for combining the results into a recommendation
> and defending the architectural claim.

## Why the Prompt Spans the Stack {#sec-why-the-prompt-spans-the-stack}

@fig-moonshot-prompt makes the scale of the lighthouse request explicit. The
prompt occupies only a few lines, but answering it requires choices about the
workload, software contract, compute organization, memory, power, compiler and
runtime, physical design, reliability, and verification. The bottom panel
shows the work required to connect those choices to tools, evidence, and an
architectural decision.

![**From design request to architecture study:** The prompt implies architecture requirements and engineering work that must be completed before an architect can formulate, explore, implement, evaluate, explain, and defend an answer.](images/F1-moonshot-stack){#fig-moonshot-prompt width="100%" fig-alt="Stacked diagram with the full lighthouse prompt at the top, architecture requirements in the middle, and the execution and analysis requirements needed to support an answer at the bottom."}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

Start with the request itself. It asks the architect to compare several compute organizations
for a real-time mobile extended-reality (XR) workload while respecting a 3 W
thermal-design-power (TDP) budget and the limits of a low-power (LP) process.
Its individual phrases bound different parts of the study. "64-bit RISC-V-based
compute subsystem" establishes the ISA, ABI, and software contract.
"XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload" sets the workload, while
"vector-capable CPU, accelerator, or SoC block" leaves several compute
organizations open for comparison. The 3 W target and 3 nm-class LP process
supply physical limits that can rule out a proposal.

The prompt asks for a design-space report, but the study may also produce
software, models, RTL, layouts, prototypes, or a chip. How far implementation
must go depends on the claim. The report connects those artifacts and
measurements to the proposed design and explains why alternatives were
rejected. The prompt deliberately leaves the accelerator microarchitecture,
memory system, software integration, and evaluation tools open. A supported
answer therefore has to connect memory, data movement, compilers, runtimes,
reliability, verification, and physical design within one architecture study.

Parts of this work are already practical. Reinforcement-learning[^fn-rl-reinforcement-learning]
systems have been used for physical-design exploration, and learned
circuit-generation methods have produced silicon evidence
[@Synopsys2023DSOai; @RoyEtAl2021PrefixRL].
@sec-loop-patterns-across-stack returns to these examples. AgentDSE uses an
LLM-based agent with simulator feedback for design-space exploration
[@WangEtAl2026AgentDSE]. These systems automate useful parts of the work, but
engineers must still connect their results, review the evidence, and take
responsibility for the resulting claim.

[^fn-rl-reinforcement-learning]: Reinforcement learning is a machine-learning method in which an agent learns to choose actions that maximize a cumulative reward signal from its environment, often used to navigate complex design spaces [@SuttonBarto2018ReinforcementLearning]. In an AI-assisted architecture study, it is one way to search. Its proposals still require the same evidence and checks as those from any other method.

Progress toward the moonshot will require more than isolated demonstrations.
SPEC-style benchmarks made performance claims more comparable by specifying
workloads, run rules, and reporting standards. Shared simulators such as gem5
made architecture experiments easier to inspect across labs. FPGA-accelerated
simulation platforms such as FireSim, community benchmark suites such as
MLPerf, artifact-evaluation practices, and open tool flows have mattered
because they let other researchers understand, rerun, challenge, and safely
build on reported results [@SPEC2017CPU; @BinkertEtAl2011Gem5;
@KarandikarEtAl2018FireSim; @MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf]. The same principle applies
here. Shared tasks, benchmarks, tool interfaces, evaluation methods, and
evidence standards can make results useful beyond a single study.

@tbl-moonshot-decomposition collects the architectural decisions implied by
the lighthouse prompt and the checks that could disqualify an answer.

| **Prompt fragment** | **Architectural decisions** | **What must be checked** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **XRBench mobile XR** | Workload slice, input distribution, quality-of-service target, latency deadline, memory traffic, software pipeline, and drift assumptions. | Trace source, benchmark version, documented workload-phase selection, and rejection when results over-optimize a single hot loop or miss real-time behavior. |
| **64-bit RISC-V with vector or accelerator option** | ISA boundary, custom extension policy, programming model, compiler/runtime path, library support, and software compatibility. | Correctness, toolchain support, generated-code evidence, portability checks, and rejection of unsupported software semantics. |
| **Compute subsystem** | CPU, fixed-function accelerator, reconfigurable spatial array (e.g., CGRA or Reconfigurable Dataflow Architecture/RDA), or Processing-in-Memory (PIM) data-centric block; memory hierarchy, SoC integration, interface compatibility, and relevant reliability or isolation assumptions. | Design-space comparison and rejection of candidates that only win by moving cost, bandwidth, energy, complexity, or risk elsewhere. |
| **3 W, 3 nm-class low-power envelope** | Power, voltage/frequency, thermal, area, EDA constraints, and dynamic power management interfaces (e.g., Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling or DVFS, power domains, thermal sensors) for the OS/firmware. | Source, version, and calibration of the power model; physical-design limits (IR drop, electromigration, timing closure); sensitivity analysis; and rejection criteria for runtime throttling or reliability failures within the scoped workload. |
| **Design-space report with evidence and rejected alternatives** | Alternatives, the Pareto frontier (the objective outcomes of feasible designs for which no alternative is at least as good on every objective and strictly better on at least one) [@Deb2001MultiObjective], assumptions, uncertainty, verification plan, rejected or failed candidates, and points at which the responsible architect decides whether to continue. | Measurements linked to assumptions and checks, rejected candidates, coverage, reproducible artifacts, and a named person responsible for the recommendation. |

: **What each prompt fragment requires:** The lighthouse prompt maps to decisions about workload definition, software and ISA contracts, hardware organization, physical feasibility, and evidence. {#tbl-moonshot-decomposition tbl-colwidths="[18,34,35]"}

The table is a first-pass checklist rather than the final review form. An
answer is incomplete if it cannot identify its workload slice, software
contract, physical assumptions, supporting observations, or decision owner.
The material from @sec-data-representations-world-models through
@sec-feedback-verification-trust develops these requirements in more detail,
and @sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card later collects them in a compact form.

::: {.callout-engineer-move title="Expand the request before evaluating it"}
Expand any compact architecture request by answering six questions. For the
lighthouse prompt, @tbl-moonshot-decomposition supplies the first answers.

1. **What architecture question is being formulated?** Name the workload,
   baseline, constraints, scope, and non-goals.
2. **Which meaningful alternatives should be explored?** Separate choices that
   could change the conclusion from trivial parameter variations.
3. **What artifacts and tool path will implement them?** Name the models,
   software, simulators, RTL, or physical-design steps needed for comparison.
4. **Which experiments could support or overturn the result?** State the
   measurements, fidelity, cost, and failure conditions.
5. **Which architectural mechanisms could explain the result?** Connect the
   measurements to workload behavior, hardware organization, and software.
6. **What recommendation can be defended?** State who is responsible for it, what its limits
   are, and what evidence would reverse it.

Until these questions are answered, the request is not ready for an
AI-assisted architecture study.
:::

Each row in the table requires further implementation and evidence work.
Meeting the "3 W, 3 nm-class" constraint requires work in RTL, synthesis,
floorplanning, timing, IR drop, leakage, thermal analysis, and signoff.
Evaluating the "RISC-V with vector or accelerator option" alternatives also
requires compiler, runtime, library, generated-code, and portability evidence.
The request is therefore a computer architecture problem rather than a prompt
engineering exercise. A supported answer must connect decisions and evidence
across the stack.

The workload and process phrases also need more detail than the compact prompt
can provide. XRBench, a benchmark suite for mobile XR workloads, supplies a
workload anchor rather than a vague application label [@KwonEtAl2023XRBench].
End-to-end XR research shows how tightly the stages from perception to display
are coupled. Mobile XR is therefore a system-level architecture target, not a
single kernel to optimize [@HuzaifaEtAl2021ILLIXR]. The process node is stated
as a class rather than as a named foundry process design kit (PDK). Current
mobile SoCs are publicly described in 3 nm-class technology, but a real study
must still name its process, libraries, voltage assumptions, and signoff path
[@Apple2024A18Pro; @Apple2024M4].

The architect might begin with an XRBench trace slice and software pipeline,
propose vector-width, local-memory, or CPU/accelerator alternatives, and screen
them with a cheap proxy. The architect can then reserve scarce compiler,
simulator, RTL, or physical-design feedback for the candidates whose results
could change the decision. That selection rule should be stated before the run,
along with what the resulting evidence may support.

Within that workflow, generation might produce legal vector configurations,
prediction might estimate their data-movement and power risks, and optimization
might choose which configurations merit expensive evaluation. A proxy may rank
the candidates one way, a simulator may overturn that ranking, and an EDA run
may return timing paths, congestion, or rule violations rather than a single
score. Search can also overfit[^fn-ml-overfitting] a convenient proxy.

Those methods can narrow the work, but their output alone does not show
whether a compiler assumption holds, whether the measured behavior follows
from the proposed mechanism, or whether the remaining tradeoff justifies a
commitment. The study should keep the candidates, assumptions, measurements,
and rejections that affect its claim. It does not need every internal model
trace. The architect must resolve the remaining questions using evidence from
the complete study.

Later chapters return to this prompt, alongside other examples better suited to
particular topics. Keeping the workload, comparison, physical envelope, and
evidence requirements fixed prevents a change in method from being mistaken
for a change in the architectural claim.

[^fn-ml-overfitting]: Overfitting, in machine learning, is fitting the noise and idiosyncrasies of a training sample so that performance fails to generalize to new data. An optimizer can overfit a proxy the same way, raising the proxy's measured score by exploiting its quirks rather than improving the true objective. Architects should therefore test a promising candidate with stronger evidence before committing to it.

## Why Efficiency Is Harder to Judge {#sec-efficiency-claims-need-rejectable-loops}

The lighthouse prompt is difficult because an efficient subsystem cannot be
judged from one score. Workload behavior, software support, data movement,
physical limits, verification effort, and deployment conditions can all change
which design is preferable. Coordinating those choices now takes more of the
architect's time and effort.

Efficiency is a claim about how well a particular architecture performs useful
work with limited resources. The claim must name the design, workload, and
resource budget it covers. A faster design has not solved the problem if it
exceeds the power budget, cannot be verified, or depends on fragile software
assumptions.

Efficiency now spans more than performance and power. Classical computer
architecture made performance quantitative while also treating cost and power
as first-class constraints
[@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach]. Dennard-style scaling once
made it easier to improve performance while keeping power density manageable
[@DennardEtAl1974Scaling]. When that scaling regime weakened, dark silicon (the
share of a chip that must stay powered off to fit its thermal budget) and the
limits of multicore scaling pushed the field toward specialization
[@BorkarChien2011FutureMicroprocessors; @EsmaeilzadehEtAl2011DarkSilicon; @HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge].
The energy cost of data movement made arithmetic alone an incomplete measure
[@Horowitz2014Energy]. Warehouse-scale operation expanded the boundary to
power delivery, utilization, networking, operations, and total cost of
ownership [@BarrosoHolzleRanganathan2019DatacenterAsComputer].
Sustainability adds operational and embodied carbon to the comparison. These
depend on energy use, hardware manufacturing and infrastructure, utilization,
geography, and system lifetime [@GuptaEtAl2021ChasingCarbon].
An AI-assisted study must state the assumptions and evidence relevant to each
efficiency dimension and identify the violations that would reject a candidate.

A valid comparison must also account for failed and rejected alternatives, not
only the winners. Keeping those results prevents later architects from repeating
work the study already ruled out and shows which parts of the design space the
claim actually covers. @sec-data-representations-world-models develops the
representation, and @sec-feedback-verification-trust develops the evidence
practice.

Modern benchmark suites already reflect this broader view. MLPerf, a community
effort to make machine-learning performance claims reproducible across
systems, includes deployment scenario, latency, throughput, accuracy, and power
in its comparisons [@MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf].
@sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales examines the numbers in detail. Useful work
must be evaluated against the resource that is scarce in the stated setting,
whether that is latency, throughput, energy, power, area, cost, carbon,
reliability, verification effort, engineering time, or risk.

The design, workload, scenario, and scarce resource together define what
"efficient" means. For the lighthouse system, a candidate that improves average
throughput may still be a poor choice if it misses an XR frame deadline, exceeds
the 3 W envelope under sustained load, or adds verification work that the
project cannot absorb. Changing any of those conditions changes the claim and
the evidence needed to support it.

@tbl-efficiency-dimensions separates six dimensions that an architecture study
may need to compare. Each study should select the dimensions that define its
claim. Reading the rows together also shows where a gain may shift cost or risk
elsewhere.

| **Dimension** | **Efficiency question** | **Why it complicates the comparison** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Performance** | How much useful work is delivered per unit time, latency budget, or service-level target? | The answer depends on workload selection, scenario, software stack, and whether the measured behavior matches the deployment claim. |
| **Power and energy** | How much useful work is delivered per watt, joule, thermal budget, or battery envelope? | The study must model activity, data movement, voltage/frequency choices, thermal constraints, and fidelity gaps between estimates and signoff. |
| **Reliability and correctness** | How much useful work survives faults, corner cases, nondeterminism, and validation? | A faster candidate is not efficient if it spends its savings on fragility, debug burden, or invalid software and hardware assumptions. |
| **Scalability and cost** | How much useful work is delivered per dollar, rack, network hop, operator action, or unit of capacity? | Local wins can shift cost to memory, network, power delivery, utilization, operations, or total cost of ownership. |
| **Sustainability** | How much useful work is delivered per unit of operational and embodied environmental footprint? | Carbon depends on hardware lifetime, manufacturing, energy mix, utilization, and where and when computation runs. |
| **Evidence and engineering effort** | How much decision-relevant evidence is obtained per simulation, experiment, verification run, or engineer-hour? | A study that generates more candidates can still be inefficient if it consumes scarce feedback, hides failures, or produces evidence that cannot reject outputs. |

: **Dimensions of an efficiency claim:** Performance, power, reliability, scalability, sustainability, and evidence cost are increasingly coupled. A valid comparison must show when an apparent improvement shifts cost or changes the scope. {#tbl-efficiency-dimensions tbl-colwidths="[22,32,34]"}

These dimensions interact. Better simulated performance can be offset by added
verification work. Lower energy may depend on fragile software assumptions,
and a candidate that wins under a proxy may fail on a more faithful workload.
The architect must explain which tradeoffs are acceptable for the stated
workload and constraints. An efficiency claim must state its scope instead of
relying on one attractive artifact or score.

These coupled requirements do not mean every study needs AI. Traditional
architecture methods remain effective when the workload, abstraction, feedback
path, and proposed change are clear. AI becomes useful when coordinating the
objectives is too slow, too implicit, or too expensive. A study should identify
which work familiar models, scripts, simulation, and expert review already
handle well, then state what additional work AI is expected to improve. The
test is whether the assistance produces a better system or a more reliable
conclusion, not simply more artifacts.

When candidate generation becomes cheap, evaluation and review may become the
bottleneck. Architects should not assume that this has happened. They should
compare how quickly valid proposals arrive with how quickly the study can test
them, reject weak ones, explain the results, and reach a decision.

The lighthouse prompt makes this concrete. The requested subsystem must
support a workload class, meet a power envelope, fit a technology assumption,
interact with software, and produce evidence. The study must compare
tradeoffs among energy, latency, memory traffic, programmability,
verification, and deployment risk. A single scalar objective may be useful
inside the study, but it cannot be the whole architectural judgment.

```{=latex}
\Needspace{20\baselineskip}
```

## The AlphaChip Debate

Researchers can reach different conclusions when they evaluate a method with
different baselines, compute budgets, implementation conditions, or downstream
metrics. The public debate over AlphaChip, a learning-based macro-placement
method, provides a well-documented example. We use it as a neutral teaching
case. Access to training inputs, the choice of proxy metric, and evidence from
reported deployment also shape the comparison. The summary below separates the
reported results and criticisms from the questions that the available public
evidence cannot settle.

::: {.callout-war-story title="Faster placement did not settle the AlphaChip comparison"}
**The claim.** In 2021, a Google team reported a reinforcement-learning method
for chip macro placement [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement]. The paper stated
that the method could generate a placement in under six hours, with results
comparable to or better than human experts on key power, performance, and area
metrics. A 2024 *Nature* addendum named the method AlphaChip
[@GoldieEtAl2024AlphaChipAddendum]. Google later reported its use across several
TPU generations [@GoogleDeepMind2024AlphaChip].

**The gap.** A 2023 independent reassessment reached a different conclusion
[@ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL]. It found that a strong simulated-annealing
baseline matched or beat the method's proxy cost on most of its modern test
cases. Commercial and analytical placers also produced lower routed wirelength
in many comparisons, and the proxy correlated weakly with final chip metrics.
The original authors challenged the reassessment
[@GoldieEtAl2024ThatChipHasSailed]. They argued that it omitted central
pretraining, stopped before convergence, used much less compute, and tested
designs they considered unrepresentative. Google later released code and a
pretrained checkpoint, but not the proprietary inputs used for pretraining.
*Nature* published a clarifying addendum in 2024
[@GoldieEtAl2024AlphaChipAddendum]. The reassessment's authors reaffirmed their
conclusions in a 2026 journal update [@ChengEtAl2026UpdatedAssessment].

**The lesson.** Google reports deployment across TPU generations, but no public
independent study has compared the complete method with strong classical
baselines under the same test cases, compute budgets, implementation
conditions, and downstream physical-design metrics. The available evidence
therefore does not settle the dispute. Reviewers need to see those experimental
differences, along with shared benchmarks and reproducible artifacts where
possible, before deciding what the comparison supports.
:::

Reported deployment is relevant evidence, but it does not by itself settle a
matched comparison with strong alternatives. Reviewers also need the benchmark,
baseline, tool path, compute budget, and downstream metrics that define the
claim. The same requirements apply to the lighthouse study.
Later chapters return to the AlphaChip case as they introduce the concepts
needed to examine each part. @sec-proxy-mismatch-metric-gaming-and-calibration
examines the relationship between proxy cost and downstream metrics, while
@sec-alphachip-public-record-audit asks what can be reconstructed from public
artifacts. Other discussions use the same case to consider matched baselines,
reproducibility, access to training inputs and tools, and the limits of public
evidence.

## Boundaries of the Argument

Models, tools, and benchmarks change quickly. This material therefore focuses
on the architecture decision each piece of work advances, the role AI plays,
and the evidence and limits that make its claim defensible.

Detailed installation instructions and tool recipes are likewise outside the
main discussion. The focus is how tools support architecture questions,
artifacts, experiments, explanations, and decisions.
@sec-appendix-a-bootstrapping gives a compact bootstrap path.
@sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card gives a compact review form and rubric.

AI participation does not remove architectural responsibility. The responsible
architect must frame the task, choose abstractions, representations, tools, and
fidelity, interpret mechanisms, set evidence standards, define what may be
automated, and remain answerable for claims that affect a design decision.
Current AI systems do not provide push-button chip design or replace the
architect. They can contribute when connected to architectural representations,
tools, and experiments, but the architect still decides what the resulting
evidence supports.
@sec-data-representations-world-models develops the artifacts used to document
that work.

The remaining chapters develop that approach. @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales
diagnoses where rising specialization and system coupling create useful roles
for AI and where they merely move the bottleneck. @sec-architecture-20-ontology
defines the study, claim, and loop, along with the information reviewers need.
@sec-data-representations-world-models and
@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces develop the representations,
data, environments, and tool interfaces needed for implementation.
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization separates method roles,
and @sec-feedback-verification-trust develops evaluation and evidence.
@sec-running-the-loop executes and analyzes the model-plus-SCALE-Sim study used
throughout this material. @sec-loop-patterns-across-stack tests transfer
across architecture layers, and @sec-what-architect-owns integrates the six
capabilities into a defensible recommendation. The appendices supply practical
review artifacts and a resource catalog.

## Open Research Questions

The following questions recur throughout this material. The discussion from
@sec-architecture-20-ontology through @sec-what-architect-owns develops the
state, environments, methods, evidence, execution, and responsibility needed to
investigate them.

1. **When is AI-assisted design work an architecture contribution?**
AI-assisted work becomes an architecture contribution when it improves what
architects can understand, build, compare, or decide under hardware/software
and physical constraints. A useful research rubric would distinguish
architectural contributions in
artifacts, methods, datasets, environments, findings, and decisions from
novelty in the AI method alone. An evaluation could test reviewer agreement on
borderline papers and show where the rubric changes review outcomes. This
question uses the
different modes of participation in @sec-from-architecture-10-to-architecture-20.

2. **How should we measure whether AI improves architectural insight?**
AI could help an architect expose an overlooked bottleneck, choose a more
informative experiment, connect measurements to mechanisms, or show why an
apparent improvement will not transfer. Evaluations should therefore test
explanation and decision quality on unfamiliar problems as well as candidate
scores. This returns to Hamming's distinction at the beginning of the chapter
between producing insight and producing more numbers.

3. **What evidence is sufficient when complete reproduction is impossible?**
Architecture results often depend on proprietary workloads, process design
kits, training inputs, tool settings, or deployed systems that outside groups
cannot inspect. Researchers need to determine what combination of public
artifacts, matched baselines, sensitivity studies, independent checks, and
limited-access review can support a bounded claim. AlphaChip makes this problem
concrete, but the same difficulty appears throughout industrial architecture
research. A useful standard should distinguish a credible deployment report,
a reproducible public comparison, and a claim that remains unresolved.

4. **How should venues review AI-assisted architecture studies?**
Architecture venues already know how to inspect artifacts, baselines, and
quantitative tradeoffs. AI-assisted studies must also expose the inputs and
artifact versions available to a method, the actions it was allowed to take,
the alternatives it rejected, and the decisions reserved for people. A paper
or community study could propose review criteria, artifact-review norms, and
call-for-papers language, then test whether reviewers identify unsupported
decisions earlier.

5. **What shared infrastructure makes progress comparable?**
If AI-assisted architecture remains a collection of isolated demonstrations,
the field will struggle to compare results. Shared problem definitions, case
studies, datasets, workload suites, environments, baselines, experiment
protocols, explanation criteria, accounts of rejected alternatives, and teaching
materials would let groups produce comparable findings without pretending that
every architecture problem has one fixed benchmark. The test is whether these
artifacts support follow-on work, negative results, and fair comparisons after
the original demonstration.

## Conclusion

The lighthouse prompt shows what it would take for AI to help an architect
formulate, explore, implement, evaluate, explain, and defend a system design
rather than settle for a plausible candidate. A complete study needs a clear
question, meaningful alternatives, checkable artifacts, experiments, a
mechanism-level explanation, and a recommendation. Readers should judge the
whole study, not only the artifact AI produced, and ask whether its evidence
supports the architectural claim.

Efficiency now couples performance, energy, power delivery, reliability,
sustainability, cost, verification effort, and the time needed to obtain useful
evidence. When plausible artifacts are cheap, evaluation may become the
expensive part of the study. Search, tool feedback, interpretation, evidence,
or review can each become the limiting resource.

The field may be entering a new era of design practice, but its direction is
not settled. AI models and tool interfaces are changing quickly, and no one yet
knows how far they can contribute to architecture work. This material offers
guidance for studies that can be run now while keeping open the questions that
future tools and evidence must answer.

The responsible architect still frames the task, chooses abstractions,
representations, tools, and experiments, explains the architectural mechanisms,
sets evidence standards, and decides what may be automated. The architect
remains responsible for the resulting claim. Keeping the inputs, results,
rejections, and decisions lets others challenge the work and improve it.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** Read any AI architecture result as a study with declared limits. Identify its question, alternatives, artifacts, experiments, mechanism-level explanation, recommendation, and evidence limits.
- **Reader test:** Can you reconstruct how AI contributed well enough to challenge the architecture claim and state what evidence would change it?
- **Up next:** @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales asks where AI genuinely improves that work and where it merely moves the bottleneck.
:::
